topic: Schema validation, transformation versioning, and drift (briefly)
scope: Kept ringkas — this is FE, not MLOps.
key_concepts: Before features enter the transformation pipeline in production, their incoming data format must be validated against an expected schema.
Transformations themselves are code and artifacts; they must be versioned alongside the models they serve to ensure reproducibility.
Data drift occurs over time when the underlying real-world distributions change, requiring pipeline retraining or feature redesign.
While MLOps systems handle the automation of these tasks, a feature engineer must design the features so they are capable of being validated, versioned, and monitored.

definitions.meaning: Checking that new data adheres to expected data types, column names, and value ranges before processing.
The phenomenon where the statistical properties of the input data change over time, degrading model accuracy.

examples: A schema validation step catches when an upstream database change suddenly outputs a numeric age column as text. Versioning ensures that if we roll back a model to v1.2, we also roll back the feature pipeline to v1.2.

relations.ch17_s02: A natural continuation of preventing skew, focusing on temporal changes rather than structural bugs.

exclusions: Do not detail specific schema tools like Great Expectations or Pydantic.
Do not detail drift detection algorithms (e.g., Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests).
